title: Quant Salary

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: Eight quants want to know the AVERAGE of their salaries -- but no one is willing to disclose their individual salary, even to the other quants.
rules: Each quant knows only their own salary.
Quants pass numbers to each other in a chain.
Each quant trusts the others not to actively cheat (no one will lie about adding their salary), but is unwilling to reveal their own salary directly.
There is no central trusted party.
setup.question: How can the 8 quants compute their average salary without revealing individual salaries?

learningObjectives: Use a random secret to BLIND a running total so intermediate values reveal nothing.
Recognize that the secret can be removed by the same person who added it -- yielding the unbiased sum.
Generalize the pattern as a primitive of secure multi-party computation.

stages.label: Why the obvious approach fails
stages.prompt: Why can't quant 1 simply add up all 8 salaries by passing the running total around the chain?

options.text: Quant 2 would see (quant 1's salary), revealing it directly.
The running total exceeds quant 1's RAM.

stages.explanation: If quant 1 passes their salary directly to quant 2, quant 2 learns it. Naive chain-summing leaks salaries to neighbors. The trick is to mask each salary so no intermediate reveals one.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Quant 1 picks a LARGE random number R, adds their salary to it, and passes the result. Each subsequent quant adds their salary. The total returns to quant 1, who subtracts R.
Each quant submits salaries to a notary.
Encrypt each salary with a public key.

stages.explanation: Random-blinding solves it. R is unknown to anyone except quant 1, so quant 2 sees only (R + salary_1) -- a number that could be anything. R cancels out only when quant 1 subtracts it at the end. This is the simplest primitive of secure multi-party computation.

stages.label: Why the blinding works
stages.prompt: From quant 2's perspective, after receiving (R + salary_1), what can they infer about salary_1?

options.text: Nothing -- R is unknown and arbitrary, so (R + salary_1) is indistinguishable from any other value.
Salary_1 directly, by subtracting a known constant.

stages.explanation: If R is uniformly random over a large range, (R + salary_1) is also uniformly random over that range. Quant 2 learns nothing about salary_1. This is the cryptographic property called 'information-theoretic hiding'.

stages.label: The cancellation step
stages.prompt: After the running total has gone around all 8 quants and returns to quant 1, the value is (R + sum_of_all_8_salaries). What does quant 1 do?

options.text: Subtract R, then divide by 8 -- and announce the result.

stages.explanation: Quant 1 knows R (the secret they added), so subtracting R yields the true sum. Dividing by 8 gives the average. Quant 1 announces only the average -- no one (not even quant 1) ever sees a specific person's salary.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: Does this protocol leak any individual salary?

options.text: No -- assuming no two quants collude, no one learns more than the average.
Yes -- the last quant in the chain knows everyone else's salary.
Yes -- quant 1 sees everyone else's salary by subtracting their own.

stages.explanation: Each quant sees only a blinded running total, which is uniformly random from their viewpoint. Quant 1 sees the total but not individual salaries. The protocol is secure if no two adjacent quants collude (which would let them subtract their two contributions and infer the gap).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What goes wrong if any TWO of the quants collude?
How would you extend this to compute MEDIAN salary instead of average? Is it possible without a trusted party?
This protocol is used in practice -- where? (Hint: data anonymization for fund position reporting.)
